branchingScenario.title: PBL: Principles of Genetics, Chromosomal Aberrations & Clinical Genetics — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 38-year-old primigravida presents for prenatal counselling. Work through the clinical reasoning to identify chromosomal abnormalities and guide genetic management.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified the chromosomal aberration, interpreted the karyotype, and guided appropriate genetic counselling. Strong grasp of clinical genetics principles.
You demonstrated some knowledge of genetics but missed key diagnostic or counselling steps. Review trisomies, sex chromosome disorders, and translocation patterns.
Review the fundamentals of chromosomal aberrations, karyotyping, and genetic counselling. Focus on Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, and Robertsonian translocations.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 38-year-old primigravida is referred to the genetics clinic after a first-trimester screening reveals increased nuchal translucency (3.8 mm) and abnormal serum markers (low PAPP-A, high free beta-hCG). She is anxious about the possibility of a chromosomal abnormality in her baby.

Key information:

  • Advanced maternal age (38 years)
  • Nuchal translucency: 3.8 mm (elevated)
  • Combined risk for trisomy 21: 1 in 50

Consider which chromosomal abnormality is most likely given these findings.

branchingQuestion.question: Based on the maternal age, elevated nuchal translucency, and abnormal serum markers, which chromosomal abnormality is most likely?

alternatives.text: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) — the most common autosomal trisomy with increased incidence at advanced maternal age

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Trisomy 21 is the most common chromosomal cause of intellectual disability. Incidence increases sharply with maternal age: ~1 in 30 at age 45. The combined first-trimester screen (nuchal translucency + serum markers) has ~90% detection rate.

alternatives.text: Turner syndrome (45,X) — a sex chromosome monosomy

feedback.title: Not the best answer
feedback.subtitle: Turner syndrome can present with increased nuchal translucency (often as cystic hygroma), but the serum marker pattern here (low PAPP-A, high beta-hCG) is more characteristic of trisomy 21. Turner syndrome typically shows low beta-hCG.

alternatives.text: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) — a sex chromosome trisomy

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Klinefelter syndrome is usually not detected prenatally through standard screening. It does not typically cause elevated nuchal translucency or the characteristic serum marker pattern seen here.

params.text:
Diagnostic Workup

Amniocentesis is performed at 16 weeks. The karyotype report returns: 47,XX,+21.

This confirms trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) — three copies of chromosome 21 due to meiotic nondisjunction, most commonly during maternal meiosis I.

The parents ask: 'If we have another child, what is the recurrence risk?'

You need to determine whether this is a free trisomy (nondisjunction) or a translocation form, as this critically affects recurrence risk and genetic counselling.

branchingQuestion.question: The karyotype shows 47,XX,+21 (free trisomy). What is the most appropriate recurrence risk counselling for this couple?

alternatives.text: Recurrence risk is approximately 1% or the age-related risk, whichever is higher — standard for free trisomy 21

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: For free trisomy 21 from nondisjunction, the empirical recurrence risk is ~1% for mothers under 35, or the age-related risk if higher. At age 38, the age-related risk (~1 in 150) is lower than 1%, so 1% is quoted. Parental karyotyping is still recommended to exclude a balanced Robertsonian translocation carrier.

alternatives.text: Recurrence risk is 100% because it is a genetic condition that will always recur

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Free trisomy 21 from nondisjunction is typically a sporadic event. The recurrence risk is low (~1%), not 100%. Only if a parent carries a 21;21 Robertsonian translocation would the recurrence be 100%.

alternatives.text: Recurrence risk is 25% following autosomal recessive inheritance pattern

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Down syndrome is a chromosomal aneuploidy, not a single-gene disorder. It does not follow Mendelian inheritance patterns. The recurrence risk for free trisomy 21 is approximately 1%.

alternatives.text: No increased recurrence risk — it was a random event with no chance of recurring

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: While nondisjunction is largely random, there IS a slightly increased recurrence risk (~1%) above the background population risk. This may relate to predisposition to nondisjunction or undetected gonadal mosaicism.

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome): Most common autosomal trisomy; 95% due to meiotic nondisjunction, 4% Robertsonian translocation, 1% mosaicism
  • Advanced maternal age: Major risk factor — risk increases from 1 in 1,500 at age 20 to 1 in 30 at age 45
  • Turner syndrome (45,X): Short stature, webbed neck, ovarian dysgenesis; most common sex chromosome abnormality in females
  • Robertsonian translocation: Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22); balanced carriers have 45 chromosomes but are phenotypically normal
  • Mosaicism: Two or more genetically distinct cell lines from post-zygotic nondisjunction; phenotype depends on proportion and distribution of abnormal cells
  • Recurrence risk: ~1% for free trisomy 21; up to 100% if parent carries a 21;21 Robertsonian translocation
